turns.text: Hello Camellia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're thinking about starting a family, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts slightly on the chair, tucking one leg under me*

Thank you for having me. And thank you for framing it that way — it actually means a lot that you're interested in the experience part, not just the medical outcomes.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

Honestly? I'm in this strange place right now. I've spent two decades understanding pregnancy at the molecular level — I can tell you exactly how maternal cortisol crosses the placental barrier and affects fetal programming — but sitting in the waiting room last month, all of that knowledge just... it didn't help. It made things harder, actually.

I think what surprised me most was how *alone* this decision feels, even though I'm surrounded by data and research. I'm forty-four, unmarried, which I know carries certain assumptions. My doctor was kind, but I could feel her cataloging my age like it was a clock counting down. And maybe it is, but I'm not here because I suddenly woke up one day wanting a baby. I'm here because I've finally built a life stable enough — emotionally, financially — to actually *be* a parent. That took time.

*meets your eyes*

What I really want from maternity care, I think, is acknowledgment that the journey here was intentional. That I'm not a cautionary tale or a checkbox for "advanced maternal age." I'm a woman who knows what can go wrong, probably better than most, and I'm choosing this anyway.

Does that make sense?
Tell me about the people who were part of your world when you first started thinking about having a baby — who came to mind, and what role did each of them play in those early conversations?

*laughs quietly, a bit ruefully*

That's a generous way to ask, because honestly? There weren't really conversations. Not the way you might imagine them.

My colleagues at the lab knew before anyone else, which is funny because they're scientists, not confidants. I remember mentioning it casually during a journal club meeting — something about reconsidering my timeline — and the room got this weird quiet. One of them, Marcus, he's been here longer than I have, he just said, "Good for you. That's brave." I think he meant the age part, or maybe the single part. I still don't know.

*fidgets with the fabric of my sleeve*

My mom... we haven't actually had *the* conversation yet. We talk around it. She keeps asking when I'm coming home for holidays, and there's this hopeful lilt in her voice that I recognize. She had me at twenty-six. I don't think she knows how to talk to me about this — like maybe she thinks I'm grieving something, or making a desperate choice. That stings a little.

And then there's my therapist, who I see every other week. She's been the most helpful, honestly. She doesn't treat this like a biological deadline. She asks me what I actually *want*, not what I should want at my age.

*looks down*

But real talk? Most people in my life don't know yet. I wanted to get through the preconception stuff first. It feels safer that way — less real, less likely to disappoint anyone if it doesn't happen.

Tell me about your partner's involvement — or the involvement of someone close to you — when you were deciding whether and when to try for a baby. How did those conversations go, and who held the most influence?

*long pause, then a careful breath*

I don't have a partner. That's... that's actually the elephant in the room that nobody quite knows how to address, right?

*shifts in seat, voice steady but quieter*

I've had relationships. Good ones, even. But they didn't lead anywhere that felt right for this — for *him or her*, I mean, not just for me. And I reached a point where I had to choose: wait for the "right" person and possibly miss the window entirely, or accept that maybe I'm going to do this differently than I imagined as a teenager.

That was harder than I expected to articulate, even to myself.

*looks up*

I've been exploring options — fertility preservation, donor pathways, co-parenting arrangements with close friends. When I mentioned it to one of my best friends, Sarah, she actually teared up. She said, "You're going to be an incredible mother because you're choosing this so deliberately." That meant more to me than she probably knows.

But here's the thing nobody really asks about: the grief mixed in with the hope. I'm not doing this despite being single. I'm doing this *as* a single person, which is different. There's mourning in that, for the partnership I thought would exist by now.

*meets your eyes directly*

My doctor at the preconception visit didn't push me on it, which I appreciated. She just wanted to know my support system. And I do have one. It's just... not the traditional one.

Think about the healthcare professionals you saw before you became pregnant — a GP, a fertility specialist, or anyone else. Tell me about those relationships. Who felt most helpful, and who felt more distant?

*leans back, considering*

Okay, so I've only had the one preconception appointment so far, but I'm already acutely aware of how these interactions land differently when you're forty-four and unmarried.

My GP — I've been seeing her for eight years — she was the one who actually encouraged me to schedule the preconception visit. That meant something. She knows my medical history, my family stuff, the anxiety I manage. When I told her I was thinking about pregnancy, she didn't do the thing where she suddenly becomes hyper-focused on my age. She just said, "Let's make sure you're set up well," and we talked about folic acid, my family history of thyroid issues, whether I wanted genetic counseling.

*fidgets with hands*

The preconception appointment itself was... mixed. My OB is probably fifteen years younger than me, which shouldn't matter, but it did. She was thorough, genuinely kind. But there's this moment where she asked about my "support system," and I could feel her mentally reorganizing how she was thinking about my case. Single, forty-four, full-time demanding job. I'm not high-risk medically, but I sensed I'd become high-risk *socially* in her eyes, you know?

*voice becomes more pointed*

What I haven't done yet is see a fertility specialist. And I'm terrified of that appointment, honestly. I feel like they'll look at my age and my marital status and write a very different story than the one I'm living.

I need someone who sees *me*, not just my timeline.

Tell me about the people — online or offline — who you turned to for information and reassurance before you got pregnant. Who was most influential, and how did their advice or stories shape what you believed about pregnancy?

*laughs, a little self-consciously*

Oh God, this is embarrassing. I'm a scientist, right? So you'd think I'd be reading peer-reviewed literature, which... I am, absolutely. But that's actually made things worse.

*rubs face*

I've been down some rabbit holes. Reddit threads about "geriatric pregnancy" — and yes, that's the actual medical term, which is delightful — where women my age are sharing their stories. Some triumphant, some heartbreaking. I read them at midnight like they're some kind of oracle that's going to tell me my future. There's this one woman, CarpoolMom2023 or something, who had a healthy baby at forty-five after three miscarriages, and I've probably read her posts fifty times. She's become weirdly real to me.

*looks down*

But then I'm also reading the actual data. Maternal age and chromosome abnormalities, declining egg quality — I *know* this stuff intimately from my research, which means I can't unsee it. My colleagues don't realize how privileged they are to be ignorant of these numbers.

*voice softens*

What's actually helped most is my therapist pointing out that I'm using information like a security blanket. Like if I just read enough studies, understand enough mechanisms, I can control the outcome. She's right, of course. The irony is not lost on me — the scientist trying to quantify the unquantifiable.

I haven't found one person offline who really gets it. That's partly why I'm here, honestly. I needed to talk to someone who studies this, not someone living it.
Who else was involved in pointing you toward those sources — a friend, a family member, or did you find them yourself?

*pauses, self-aware smile*

No, that's... that's all me. Self-directed spiral, basically.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I think that's actually part of the problem. I've been treating this like a research project I'm running solo. Late at night, I'm Googling things, clicking through fertility forums, cross-referencing studies I can access through the hospital database. It's very solitary.

Sarah — my friend I mentioned — she's aware I'm doing *something*, but I don't think she realizes how deep I've gone into the information vortex. Last month she said, "You're overthinking this," and I just... didn't tell her about the spreadsheet I'd made tracking optimal fertility windows and the metabolic impacts of maternal stress on fetal development.

*laughs, slightly embarrassed*

That's the scientist in me, right? I'm comfortable with data. I'm not comfortable asking for help or admitting uncertainty. So instead of calling someone and saying, "Hey, I'm scared," I read another study. I find another data point that might shore up the narrative I'm building.

*meets your eyes*

My therapist pointed out that I haven't actually asked anyone — not really — to walk through this with me. I've built this entirely in my head, alone in the lab or at my apartment at 2 AM. Which is fine, I guess, but it also means nobody really knows what I'm carrying. They see the decision. They don't see the weight of it.

I'm realizing that might be something I need to change.

Tell me about the women in your family — your mother, grandmothers, sisters, or aunts — and how their experiences of pregnancy and motherhood influenced your own thinking before you even started trying.

*takes a deep breath, clearly touched by the question*

My mother... she's the main character in this story, I think, even though she doesn't know it yet.

She had me at twenty-six. Easy pregnancy, she's told me a hundred times. She makes it sound like something that just happened to her, like the sun rose and suddenly there I was. My father was there, they were married, it all made sense. But even as a kid, I picked up on this undercurrent of — I don't know, resignation? She gave up a graduate program in education. Never went back.

*voice becomes quieter*

I watched her become a mother first, and everything else second. That wasn't her intention, but that's how it landed. And I think I made a vow, very young, that I would never do that. I would be the scientist first, secure in that identity, and *then* figure out the rest. Which is probably why I'm forty-four.

*looks up*

My grandmother — Mom's mother — she had four children and seemed genuinely content with that. But she also had no choice about it, really. It was just what women did. She passed when I was twenty-two, so I never got to ask her the hard questions.

And I don't have sisters. It's just me and my mother, this strange orbit we maintain.

*pauses, vulnerability showing*

I think part of why I haven't told her yet is because I'm terrified she'll either try to protect me from the same sacrifices she made, or worse — that she'll be disappointed it took me this long to want what she had so easily.
How did their stories — whether positive or difficult — show up in your own expectations or fears?

*sits quietly for a moment, then speaks slowly*

I think I internalized this idea that motherhood was either everything or nothing. That you either surrendered to it completely, like my mom did, or you stayed vigilant, protected yourself, kept it at arm's length. There was no middle ground in my imagination.

*voice becomes more introspective*

My mother's story taught me that pregnancy and motherhood could swallow you whole if you weren't careful. She was so capable, so intelligent, and then suddenly she was just... managing logistics. Endless logistics. I watched her light dim a little. So I built this fortress around myself — career, independence, control. I told myself I didn't need that, didn't want that.

*fidgets with hands*

But what I didn't account for is that the fortress gets lonely. And that maybe, just maybe, I could do it differently than she did. That I could be a mother *and* still be myself. That I have resources she didn't have — financial stability, education, choice.

*voice wavers slightly*

My fear now is the opposite of hers. I'm terrified I'll become so obsessed with *not* losing myself that I won't actually surrender to motherhood when it happens. That I'll stay locked in this analytical mode, observing my child's development like a lab specimen instead of just... being present with them.

*looks directly at you*

And there's this deeper fear, too. That I waited so long because some part of me didn't think I deserved it. That I'm doing this at forty-four not out of choice, but out of defiance against my own doubts.
Tell me about a time — if there was one — when trying to conceive felt harder than you expected. Who was around you during that period, and how did those relationships change or hold steady?

*shifts, a slightly uncomfortable laugh*

I haven't actually started trying yet. That's the thing — I'm still in the preconception phase, just gathering information, getting my body ready. But I think you're asking something deeper than that, aren't you?

*pauses, considering*

I guess the hardest part has been the *waiting* itself. The decision is made, but nothing can happen until... it happens. And in that space, I've realized how isolated I am with this. Last month, I had a moment — completely mundane, I was at the grocery store — where I saw a woman with a toddler, and the kid was having a meltdown in the cereal aisle. And this woman just... sat down next to her child and waited it out. So patient. So present.

*voice catches slightly*

And I thought, "I don't know if I can do that." Not the patience part — I mean, the *visibility* of it. Being a single mother, having people watch me do this, judge me for it. And I realized I've been waiting not just for the right time, but for courage I'm not sure I have.

*looks away*

Sarah was with me that day, actually. She bought me coffee without asking, and we didn't talk about it. But she knew something shifted. She's been steady — she hasn't pressured me or asked a million questions. But I also haven't told her how scared I am, which means she can't really hold that with me.

*quietly*

That's the hardest part. The loneliness of carrying this alone while people who love me stand nearby, not quite invited in.
Who else was involved in supporting you — or perhaps adding to the pressure — at that time?

*sits with that for a moment, then shakes head slowly*

Honestly? That's the problem. Nobody was really involved, because I didn't let them be.

*voice becomes more reflective*

My GP knew I was thinking about preconception planning, but I framed it very clinically — just scheduling the appointment, crossing boxes. My therapist knows the emotional landscape, but even with her, I'm careful. I present the anxieties in a very organized way, like they're research questions to be solved rather than feelings I'm actually drowning in.

*pauses*

And my mom... I've been actively *avoiding* her involvement. I think I'm afraid that if I tell her, it becomes real in a way I'm not ready for. It also becomes *her* story too — her grandchild, her legacy. And I'm not sure I have the bandwidth to manage both my own fears and whatever she projects onto this.

*looks down*

Sarah keeps trying to be more involved, I think. She'll ask gentle questions — "How are you feeling about everything?" — and I'll give her a surface answer. She's offering support, but I'm not actually *accepting* it. I'm holding everyone at a certain distance.

*voice becomes quieter*

What I'm realizing, talking to you, is that there's no pressure from outside. The pressure is all internal, all mine. And maybe that's the real isolation — not that people aren't supporting me, but that I've made it nearly impossible for them to try.

*meets your eyes*

That's something I need to fix before I actually get pregnant.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, collecting thoughts*

I think... yes, actually. There's something I keep coming back to that we haven't quite touched on.

*leans forward slightly*

All of this — the research, the planning, the obsessing over epigenetics and maternal stress and optimal conditions — it's coming from a place of love, right? But it's also coming from this deep belief that I have to *earn* the right to be a mother. That I have to be perfect, or at least perfectly prepared, to deserve this.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

And I wonder if that's something maternity care could actually address. Not just the medical side, but the psychological side. There's this assumption that by the time you're forty-four and choosing this deliberately, you've got it all figured out. You're supposed to be confident. But I'm terrified, and I don't know if there's space in the healthcare system to actually *say* that without being pathologized as high-risk or anxious or — worse — selfish for doing this alone.

*pauses*

I need someone to tell me that my body at forty-four, my mind with all its complications, my life as a single woman — that this is enough. Not in spite of those things, but including them. I need care that doesn't just manage the biology but actually sees the *person* carrying it.

*looks directly at you*

And I think there are other women like me out there. Older, single, educated, terrified, trying to do this right. We need someone to tell us it's okay to not have all the answers.
